Sri Vishnu Sahasranama

A thousand doors into the all-pervading Lord

This companion gives each name a short meaning, a theological reason for its presence in the hymn, a practical way to live with it, and a connected Puranic or epic episode. The main body follows the thousand-name recension; the appendix adds the eight concluding weapon-names commonly recited in namavali form.

How to use it Read slowly, chant a few names, then carry one reflection into your day.
Interpretation style Devotional and practical, not a replacement for full traditional bhashyas.
Story notes Closely related names sometimes share an episode because the same divine act radiates several meanings.
1008 entries

Names 1-100

1 viśvam विश्वम्

Meaning: Who is the universe himself.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

2 viṣṇuḥ विष्णुः

Meaning: He who pervades everywhere.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

3 vaṣaṭkāraḥ वषट्कारः

Meaning: On whose account oblations are offered in fire.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

4 bhūta-bhavya-bhavat-prabhuḥ भूतभव्यभवत्प्रभुः

Meaning: The lord of past, present and future.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

5 bhūtakṛt भूतकृत्

Meaning: Creator of all creatures.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

6 bhūtabhṛt भूतभृत्

Meaning: The sustainer of all creatures.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

7 bhāvaḥ भावः

Meaning: He who is in the form of pure emotions.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

8 bhūtātmā भूतात्मा

Meaning: The soul of all beings.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

9 bhūtabhāvanaḥ भूतभावनः

Meaning: He who nurtures all beings.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

10 pūtātmā पूतात्मा

Meaning: He who has a pure self.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

11 paramātmā परमात्मा

Meaning: The supreme soul.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

12 muktānāṁ parama-gatiḥ मुक्तानां परमागतिः

Meaning: The ultimate goal of all liberated souls.

Why this name may be here: Liberating names appear because the hymn is not only praise; it is also a map of the soul's final destination.

How we relate: Relate to it by asking what you are clinging to that cannot finally satisfy; the name points beyond short-lived victories.

Story: Dhruva seeks a kingdom but receives direct vision and a transformed heart; this name matches the Lord who turns ambition toward liberation.

13 avyayaḥ अव्ययः

Meaning: He who is above destruction or change.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

14 puruṣaḥ पुरुषः

Meaning: The supreme spirit.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

15 sākṣī साक्षी

Meaning: The witness.

Why this name may be here: Interior names show Vishnu as the still center discovered in discipline, attention, and inward purification.

How we relate: Relate to it through breath, restraint, silence, and attention; interior order makes devotion less reactive and more stable.

Story: Dhruva's tapas steadies the whole cosmos and culminates in Vishnu's vision; this name fits the Lord discovered through unwavering concentration.

16 kṣetrajñaḥ क्षेत्रज्ञः

Meaning: The knower of the field.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Krishna instructs Arjuna on the battlefield, turning despair into discernment; this name belongs to the Lord who teaches inside crisis.

17 akṣaraḥ अक्षरः

Meaning: He who is immutable.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

18 yogaḥ योगः

Meaning: He who is realized through yoga.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes contemplative epithets to unite devotion with self-mastery; the Lord is encountered by a gathered mind.

How we relate: It becomes practical when you choose pause over impulse and remembrance over distraction.

Story: Narada's instruction turns grief and restlessness into focused devotion; this epithet suits the inner guidance of spiritual discipline.

19 yogavidāṁ netā योगविदां नेता

Meaning: The leader of those who know yoga.

Why this name may be here: Interior names show Vishnu as the still center discovered in discipline, attention, and inward purification.

How we relate: It becomes practical when you choose pause over impulse and remembrance over distraction.

Story: The sages who meditate upon Narayana in Badarika embody this name as contemplative steadiness directed toward the supreme person.

20 pradhāna-puruṣeśvaraḥ प्रधानपुरुषेश्वरः

Meaning: The lord of matter and spirit.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

21 nārasiṁha-vapuḥ नारसिंहवपुः

Meaning: He whose form is man-lion.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Prahlada's unwavering faith draws Narasimha from the pillar, making this name the triumph of omnipresent grace over engineered cruelty.

22 śrīmān श्रीमान्

Meaning: He who always has Sri with him.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

23 keśavaḥ केशवः

Meaning: He who has beautiful hair.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: Varaha lifting the earth from cosmic waters makes elemental imagery literal; this name suits Vishnu as guardian of the world-body.

24 puruṣottamaḥ पुरुषोत्तमः

Meaning: The supreme puruṣa.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

25 sarvaḥ सर्वः

Meaning: He who is all.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

26 śarvaḥ शर्वः

Meaning: The auspicious one.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: In Tirumala lore, Srinivasa receives and protects the wealth of devotion offered by countless pilgrims; such narratives illuminate auspicious names.

27 śivaḥ शिवः

Meaning: The ever pure one.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

28 sthāṇuḥ स्थाणुः

Meaning: The pillar, the immovable truth.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: As Hayagriva, Vishnu restores the Vedas after they are stolen; the story suits names tied to knowledge and sacred speech.

29 bhūtādiḥ भूतादिः

Meaning: The origin of all beings.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

30 nidhir-avyayaḥ निधिरव्ययः

Meaning: The imperishable treasure.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes these names to tell Yudhishthira that refuge in Vishnu is not temporary relief but the road to lasting freedom.

How we relate: Carry it by practicing detachment with love, not indifference.

Story: Gajendra's rescue is not merely escape from a crocodile but deliverance from bondage and forgetfulness; this epithet fits that deeper release.

31 sambhavaḥ सम्भवः

Meaning: He who manifests by his own will.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

32 bhāvanaḥ भावनः

Meaning: He who generates noble feelings.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

33 bhartā भर्ता

Meaning: He who supports the universe.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

34 prabhavaḥ प्रभवः

Meaning: The source from which all arises.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

35 prabhuḥ प्रभुः

Meaning: The all-powerful lord.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

36 īśvaraḥ ईश्वरः

Meaning: The ruler.

Why this name may be here: Time-bearing names show that Vishnu is praised as the measure behind change, decay, renewal, and moral consequence.

How we relate: Relate to it by accepting rhythm: discipline, patience, and timely action are small human reflections of cosmic order.

Story: The cyclic creation and dissolution described in the Puranas show kalpas moving within Narayana's will; this name belongs to the Lord behind vast rhythms.

37 svayambhūḥ स्वयम्भूः

Meaning: Self-existent.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

38 śambhuḥ शम्भुः

Meaning: The source of happiness.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

39 ādityaḥ आदित्यः

Meaning: The son of Aditi.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

40 puṣkarākṣaḥ पुष्कराक्षः

Meaning: The lotus-eyed one.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: Carry it by making your surroundings and conduct worthy of the presence of Sri.

Story: Rukmini's heart turns unwaveringly toward Krishna before their marriage; this name reflects divine beauty that draws love by its own worth.

41 mahāsvanaḥ महास्वनः

Meaning: He of the mighty sound.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

42 anādi-nidhanaḥ अनादिनिधनः

Meaning: Without beginning and end.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

43 dhātā धाता

Meaning: The supporter.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

44 vidhātā विधाता

Meaning: The law-giver.

Why this name may be here: Time-bearing names show that Vishnu is praised as the measure behind change, decay, renewal, and moral consequence.

How we relate: Use this name when restless about results; it teaches that maturation has its season and cannot be bullied into ripeness.

Story: Krishna delays open confrontation with Kamsa until the right moment, showing time used as instrument rather than obstacle.

45 dhātur-uttamaḥ धातुरुत्तमः

Meaning: Higher than all causes.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

46 aprameyaḥ अप्रमेयः

Meaning: Immeasurable.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

47 hṛṣīkeśaḥ हृषीकेशः

Meaning: The lord of the senses.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

48 padmanābhaḥ पद्मनाभः

Meaning: He from whose navel springs the lotus.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

49 amara-prabhuḥ अमरप्रभुः

Meaning: Lord of the immortals.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

50 viśvakarmā विश्वकर्मा

Meaning: Architect of the universe.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

51 manuḥ मनुः

Meaning: The thinker.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

52 tvaṣṭā त्वष्टा

Meaning: The divine artisan.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

53 sthaviṣṭhaḥ स्थविष्ठः

Meaning: The most massive.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: This name becomes real when ego yields a little and action is done with sacred intention rather than constant self-display.

Story: As Yajna in primordial times, Vishnu upholds cosmic order through sacrificial harmony; the name fits the deity who is both rite and fruit.

54 sthaviro dhruvaḥ स्थविरो ध्रुवः

Meaning: The ancient and steady one.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

55 agrāhyaḥ अग्राह्यः

Meaning: He who cannot be grasped by the senses.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

56 śāśvataḥ शाश्वतः

Meaning: The eternal one.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

57 kṛṣṇaḥ कृष्णः

Meaning: The dark-hued one.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

58 lohitākṣaḥ लोहिताक्षः

Meaning: The red-eyed one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

59 pratardanaḥ प्रतर्दनः

Meaning: The destroyer at dissolution.

Why this name may be here: This name stresses that divine strength is meaningful because it rescues the vulnerable and re-orders what has gone crooked.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: Narasimha bursts forth to save Prahlada and break tyrannical pride; this name fits the Lord whose protection arrives exactly where hope seems blocked.

60 prabhūtaḥ प्रभूतः

Meaning: Ever full and abundant.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

61 trikakub-dhāmaḥ त्रिककुब्धाम

Meaning: The support of the three regions.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

62 pavitram पवित्रम्

Meaning: The purifier.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

63 maṅgalaṁ param मङ्गलं परम्

Meaning: The supreme auspiciousness.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: Relate to it by caring for beauty, cleanliness, gratitude, and generosity; auspiciousness grows where life is ordered and respectful.

Story: When Sudama visits Krishna in poverty and returns to quiet abundance, the story shows auspicious grace arriving without humiliation.

64 īśānaḥ ईशानः

Meaning: The controller.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

65 prāṇadaḥ प्राणदः

Meaning: The giver of life.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

66 prāṇaḥ प्राणः

Meaning: Life-breath itself.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

67 jyeṣṭhaḥ ज्येष्ठः

Meaning: The eldest.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

68 śreṣṭhaḥ श्रेष्ठः

Meaning: The best.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

69 prajāpatiḥ प्रजापतिः

Meaning: Lord of all beings.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

70 hiraṇyagarbhaḥ हिरण्यगर्भः

Meaning: The golden womb.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

71 bhūgarbhaḥ भूगर्भः

Meaning: He who bears the earth within.

Why this name may be here: Elemental names reveal that the sacred is not outside creation; mountains, waters, light, and life-currents all mirror Vishnu's presence.

How we relate: This name is lived when gratitude replaces casual consumption and the natural world is treated as sacred participation, not raw material only.

Story: Kurma supports Mount Mandara during the churning, turning mountain and ocean alike into instruments of divine purpose.

72 mādhavaḥ माधवः

Meaning: Consort of Ma, or lord of knowledge.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Kapila teaches Devahuti the way of liberation and right seeing; this name resonates with Vishnu as clarifying intelligence.

73 madhusūdanaḥ मधुसूदनः

Meaning: Slayer of Madhu.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Krishna slays the horse-demon Keshi, making certain victory names glow with youthful yet effortless power.

74 īśvaraḥ ईश्वरः

Meaning: The sovereign lord.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

75 vikramī विक्रमी

Meaning: The valiant one.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

76 dhanvī धन्वी

Meaning: The wielder of the bow.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma closes and intensifies the hymn with martial imagery because Vishnu's compassion sometimes arrives as sharp correction.

How we relate: Use this name when inner enemies feel stronger than outer ones; it reminds you that divine help can be sharp and cleansing.

Story: Sudarshana pursues Durvasa to defend Ambarisha, showing that divine weaponry protects bhakti rather than egoic ambition.

77 medhāvī मेधावी

Meaning: Supremely intelligent.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

78 vikramaḥ विक्रमः

Meaning: He of mighty stride.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Rama's exile keeps truth to his father's word above comfort, making righteous names shine with moral gravity.

79 kramaḥ क्रमः

Meaning: Order, progression.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: The bridge to Lanka shows leadership that gathers many beings into one dharmic purpose.

80 anuttamaḥ अनुत्तमः

Meaning: None is higher than him.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

81 durādharṣaḥ दुराधर्षः

Meaning: Impossible to overcome.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

82 kṛtajñaḥ कृतज्ञः

Meaning: Knower of all acts.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

83 kṛtiḥ कृतिः

Meaning: He who is action itself.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

84 ātmavān आत्मवान्

Meaning: Possessed of the highest self-mastery.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

85 sureśaḥ सुरेशः

Meaning: Lord of the devas.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

86 śaraṇam शरणम्

Meaning: The refuge.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

87 śarma शर्म

Meaning: Bliss and protection.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

88 viśvaretāḥ विश्वरेताः

Meaning: The seed of the universe.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

89 prajābhavaḥ प्रजाभवः

Meaning: Origin of all creatures.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

90 ahaḥ अहः

Meaning: Radiant like the day.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

91 saṁvatsaraḥ संवत्सरः

Meaning: The year, the cycle of time.

Why this name may be here: Time-bearing names show that Vishnu is praised as the measure behind change, decay, renewal, and moral consequence.

How we relate: Carry it by living with accountability, because a universe held by order also remembers consequence.

Story: Bhishma himself waits for the auspicious time of uttarayana before departing, a fitting narrative backdrop for names of divine timing and order.

92 vyālaḥ व्यालः

Meaning: Unfathomable like a serpent.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: Matsya guides the ark through the deluge, showing the Lord present in waters both destructive and protective.

93 pratyayaḥ प्रत्ययः

Meaning: The ground of certainty.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

94 sarvadarśanaḥ सर्वदर्शनः

Meaning: The all-seeing one.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

Meaning: Unborn.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

Meaning: Lord of all.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

99 sarvādiḥ सर्वादिः

Meaning: The beginning of all.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

101 vṛṣākapiḥ वृषाकपिः

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

102 ameyātmā अमेयात्मा

Meaning: Of immeasurable self.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

103 sarvayoga-viniḥsṛtaḥ सर्वयोगविनिःसृतः

Meaning: Beyond all bonds and conditions.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes contemplative epithets to unite devotion with self-mastery; the Lord is encountered by a gathered mind.

How we relate: Use this name when the senses scatter; it gathers the mind back toward the witnessing center.

Story: Krishna's teaching on mastery of mind to Arjuna reveals yoga not as escape but as disciplined God-centeredness.

104 vasuḥ वसुः

Meaning: The indwelling wealth.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

105 vasumanāḥ वसुमनाः

Meaning: He of noble mind.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

106 satyaḥ सत्यः

Meaning: Truth itself.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

107 samātmā समात्मा

Meaning: Equal in all.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

111 puṇḍarīkākṣaḥ पुण्डरीकाक्षः

Meaning: Lotus-eyed one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: Carry it by making your surroundings and conduct worthy of the presence of Sri.

Story: The Lord seated with Sri on his chest symbolizes a kingship inseparable from compassion and harmony; this name points to that inseparable splendor.

112 vṛṣakarmā वृषकर्मा

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

121 varārohaḥ वरारोहः

Meaning: Of supreme ascent.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

122 mahātapāḥ महातपाः

Meaning: Of great austerity.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

123 sarvagaḥ सर्वगः

Meaning: Present everywhere.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

124 sarvavid-bhānuḥ सर्वविद्भानुः

Meaning: The luminous knower of all.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

125 viṣvaksenaḥ विष्वक्सेनः

Meaning: He whose army is everywhere.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

126 janārdanaḥ जनार्दनः

Meaning: He whom people seek, or who corrects people.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

127 vedaḥ वेदः

Meaning: The Veda itself.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

128 vedavit वेदवित्

Meaning: Knower of the Veda.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Akrura's pilgrimage to meet Krishna transforms expectation into revelation; this name fits wisdom that descends as vision, not abstraction alone.

129 avyaṅgaḥ अव्यङ्गः

Meaning: Without imperfection.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

130 vedāṅgaḥ वेदाङ्गः

Meaning: Limb of the Veda.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: Sanatkumara and the sages seek the supreme truth and find it centered in Narayana; the name suits the Lord who is both teacher and taught-of.

131 vedavit वेदवित्

Meaning: Knower of Vedic truth.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: In the Gajendra episode, the elephant's past-life memory of prayer returns at the crucial moment; this name reflects divine knowledge awakening dormant wisdom.

132 kaviḥ कविः

Meaning: The seer.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: Krishna instructs Arjuna on the battlefield, turning despair into discernment; this name belongs to the Lord who teaches inside crisis.

133 lokādhyakṣaḥ लोकाध्यक्षः

Meaning: Overseer of the worlds.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: As Hayagriva, Vishnu restores the Vedas after they are stolen; the story suits names tied to knowledge and sacred speech.

134 surādhyakṣaḥ सुराध्यक्षः

Meaning: Overseer of the devas.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: Kapila teaches Devahuti the way of liberation and right seeing; this name resonates with Vishnu as clarifying intelligence.

135 dharmādhyakṣaḥ धर्माध्यक्षः

Meaning: Overseer of dharma.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

136 kṛtākṛtaḥ कृताकृतः

Meaning: Cause and effect.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

137 caturātmā चतुरात्मा

Meaning: Fourfold in manifestation.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

138 caturvyūhaḥ चतुर्व्यूहः

Meaning: Of four emanations.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

139 caturdaṁṣṭraḥ चतुर्दंष्ट्रः

Meaning: Four-fanged.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

140 caturbhujaḥ चतुर्भुजः

Meaning: Four-armed.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

141 bhrājiṣṇuḥ भ्राजिष्णुः

Meaning: Effulgent.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

142 bhojanam भोजनम्

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

143 bhoktā भोक्ता

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

144 sahiṣṇuḥ सहिष्णुः

Meaning: Forbearing.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

145 jagad-ādijaḥ जगदादिजः

Meaning: Born before the world.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

146 anaghaḥ अनघः

Meaning: Sinless.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

147 vijayaḥ विजयः

Meaning: Ever victorious.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes guarding and conquering names to show that compassion in Vishnu is active; it confronts chaos rather than merely overlooking it.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: Krishna lifts Govardhana to shelter Vraja from Indra's storm; this name belongs to divine strength expressed as refuge.

148 jetā जेता

Meaning: The conqueror.

Why this name may be here: Names of protection show why devotees trust Vishnu: his greatness is not distant power but power that bends toward preserving dharma.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: Draupadi's helpless cry draws Krishna's intervention in the assembly; the name suits the Lord who protects dignity when human power fails.

149 viśvayoniḥ विश्वयोनिः

Meaning: Source of the universe.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

150 punarvasuḥ पुनर्वसुः

Meaning: He who dwells again and again in beings.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

151 upendraḥ उपेन्द्रः

Meaning: Younger brother of Indra.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana arrives as a small brahmacharin before Bali, showing how divine vastness can choose apparent littleness.

152 vāmanaḥ वामनः

Meaning: The dwarf incarnation.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: The three strides that reclaim the worlds make pervasion, measure, and sovereignty visible at once.

153 prāṁśuḥ प्रांशुः

Meaning: Tall and vast.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

154 amoghaḥ अमोघः

Meaning: Whose acts are never futile.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Meaning: Pure.

156 ūrjitaḥ ऊर्जितः

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

157 atīndraḥ अतीन्द्रः

Meaning: Beyond Indra.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

158 saṅgrahaḥ सङ्ग्रहः

Meaning: The gatherer, integrator.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

160 dhṛtātmā धृतात्मा

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

161 niyamaḥ नियमः

Meaning: Order and discipline.

Why this name may be here: This epithet reminds the listener that the Lord is not hurried by time because time itself moves within his ordinance.

How we relate: Carry it by living with accountability, because a universe held by order also remembers consequence.

Story: Rama's exile ripens many destinies through disciplined endurance; this name suits a Lord whose order unfolds through season and patience.

162 yamaḥ यमः

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

177 anirdeśya-vapuḥ अनिर्देश्यवपुः
