402 śaktimatāṁ śreṣṭhaḥ शक्तिमतां श्रेष्ठः

Meaning: Best among the powerful.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

403 dharmaḥ धर्मः

Meaning: Righteousness itself.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

404 dharmavid-uttamaḥ धर्मविदुत्तमः

Meaning: The highest knower of dharma.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Kapila teaches Devahuti the way of liberation and right seeing; this name resonates with Vishnu as clarifying intelligence.

405 vaikuṇṭhaḥ वैकुण्ठः

Meaning: The unobstructed Lord of Vaikuntha.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

406 puruṣaḥ पुरुषः

Meaning: The cosmic person.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

407 prāṇaḥ प्राणः

Meaning: Life force.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

408 prāṇadaḥ प्राणदः

Meaning: Giver of life.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

409 praṇavaḥ प्रणवः

Meaning: The sacred syllable Om.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

410 pṛthuḥ पृथुः

Meaning: Vast and expansive.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

411 hiraṇyagarbhaḥ हिरण्यगर्भः

Meaning: The golden womb of creation.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

412 śatrughnaḥ शत्रुघ्नः

Meaning: Destroyer of enemies.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes guarding and conquering names to show that compassion in Vishnu is active; it confronts chaos rather than merely overlooking it.

How we relate: Repeat this name when fear rises, then ask where you can act with courage rather than collapse into helplessness.

Story: Narasimha bursts forth to save Prahlada and break tyrannical pride; this name fits the Lord whose protection arrives exactly where hope seems blocked.

413 vyāptaḥ व्याप्तः

Meaning: All-pervading.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

414 vāyuḥ वायुः

Meaning: The life-breathing wind.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: In the forest episodes of the Ramayana, trees, rivers, and mountains become witnesses to Rama's dharma; this name turns the landscape into theology.

415 adhokṣajaḥ अधोक्षजः

Meaning: Beyond the reach of the senses.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

416 ṛtuḥ ऋतुः

Meaning: Sacred season, cosmic rhythm.

Why this name may be here: This epithet reminds the listener that the Lord is not hurried by time because time itself moves within his ordinance.

How we relate: Carry it by living with accountability, because a universe held by order also remembers consequence.

Story: The return of the monsoon after Govardhana's lesson reminds Indra that power too has a rightful rhythm under higher law.

417 sudarśanaḥ सुदर्शनः

Meaning: Of beautiful vision; the discus-bearing Lord.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shows that the Lord's instruments are extensions of protection, justice, and awakened order.

How we relate: Use this name when inner enemies feel stronger than outer ones; it reminds you that divine help can be sharp and cleansing.

Story: In the war against Narakasura, Vishnu's weapons restore balance by removing predatory power; this name fits that corrective force.

418 kālaḥ कालः

Meaning: Time itself.

Why this name may be here: This epithet reminds the listener that the Lord is not hurried by time because time itself moves within his ordinance.

How we relate: Relate to it by accepting rhythm: discipline, patience, and timely action are small human reflections of cosmic order.

Story: Krishna's promise that dharma is restored age after age frames this name in the long continuity of divine intervention.

419 parameṣṭhī परमेष्ठी

Meaning: Seated in the highest place.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: Varaha lifting the earth from cosmic waters makes elemental imagery literal; this name suits Vishnu as guardian of the world-body.

420 parigrahaḥ परिग्रहः

Meaning: The receiver who gathers all unto himself.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

421 ugraḥ उग्रः

Meaning: Terrible to the wicked.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

422 saṁvatsaraḥ संवत्सरः

Meaning: The year, cyclical time.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma uses such names to place human anxiety inside a larger order governed by intelligence rather than accident.

How we relate: Relate to it by accepting rhythm: discipline, patience, and timely action are small human reflections of cosmic order.

Story: The cyclic creation and dissolution described in the Puranas show kalpas moving within Narayana's will; this name belongs to the Lord behind vast rhythms.

423 dakṣaḥ दक्षः

Meaning: Skillful and efficient.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

424 viśrāmaḥ विश्रामः

Meaning: Rest and repose.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: The defeat of Ravana manifests justice that answers long-standing misuse of power.

425 viśvadakṣiṇaḥ विश्वदक्षिणः

Meaning: The all-offering Lord to whom all gifts go.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: This name becomes real when ego yields a little and action is done with sacred intention rather than constant self-display.

Story: When Krishna teaches that offerings made with devotion are accepted even if simple, this epithet becomes inward and universal.

426 vistāraḥ विस्तारः

Meaning: Expansion, vast spread.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

427 sthāvarasthāṇuḥ स्थावरस्थाणुः

Meaning: Stable amidst all that moves and stands.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

428 pramāṇam प्रमाणम्

Meaning: The standard and proof.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Sabari's darshana shows Rama's tenderness toward humble devotion.

429 bījam-avyayam बीजमव्ययम्

Meaning: The imperishable seed.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shifts attention from worldly success to the deeper question of what finally releases sorrow.

How we relate: Use this name in moments of grief or exhaustion; it turns the heart toward the freedom that survives change.

Story: Uddhava is guided beyond grief by Krishna's final teachings; the name suits liberation through understanding and surrender.

430 arthaḥ अर्थः

Meaning: True meaning and value.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

431 anarthaḥ अनर्थः

Meaning: Beyond dependence on worldly gain.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

432 mahākośaḥ महाकोशः

Meaning: Great treasury.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

433 mahābhogaḥ महाभोगः

Meaning: Great enjoyer and source of delight.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

434 mahādhanaḥ महाधनः

Meaning: Great wealth.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

435 anirviṇṇaḥ अनिर्विण्णः

Meaning: Never despondent.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

436 sthaviṣṭhaḥ स्थविष्ठः

Meaning: The most massive and firm.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: Bali's gift to Vamana turns a ritual act into total surrender; this name fits the Lord who receives both offering and offerer.

437 bhūḥ भूः

Meaning: The earth; pure being.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: Kurma supports Mount Mandara during the churning, turning mountain and ocean alike into instruments of divine purpose.

438 dharmayūpaḥ धर्मयूपः

Meaning: The sacrificial post of righteousness.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

439 mahāmakhaḥ महामखः

Meaning: The great sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: This name becomes real when ego yields a little and action is done with sacred intention rather than constant self-display.

Story: The sages at Naimisharanya keep sacrificial time alive through remembrance of Narayana; the name belongs to the divine heart of ritual continuity.

440 nakṣatranemiḥ नक्षत्रनेमिः

Meaning: Axis of the stars.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

441 nakṣatrī नक्षत्री

Meaning: Lord of the constellations.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

442 kṣamaḥ क्षमः

Meaning: Patient and forgiving.

Why this name may be here: This name shows that Vishnu's benevolence is not sentimental; it is the steady willingness to receive even the imperfect devotee.

How we relate: Relate to it by softening judgment without lowering standards; true kindness combines warmth with steadiness.

Story: Vidura receives Krishna with love unmeasured by status, and Krishna accepts him completely; this name belongs to that nearness.

443 kṣāmaḥ क्षामः

Meaning: Lean through austerity, or subtle.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

444 samīhanaḥ समीहनः

Meaning: He of deliberate striving.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

445 yajñaḥ यज्ञः

Meaning: Sacrifice itself.

Why this name may be here: Such names connect temple, fire, mantra, and surrender into a single vision centered on Vishnu.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: In Ambarisha's observance of Ekadashi and Dvadashi, discipline itself becomes worship; this name suits Vishnu as the fulfiller of vowed offering.

446 ijyaḥ इज्यः

Meaning: He who is worthy of worship.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

447 mahejyaḥ महेज्यः

Meaning: Most worthy of worship.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

448 kratuḥ क्रतुः

Meaning: Sacred rite and resolve.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

449 satram सत्रम्

Meaning: The great sacrificial session.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

450 satāṁ gatiḥ सतां गतिः

Meaning: Goal of the good.

Why this name may be here: Liberating names appear because the hymn is not only praise; it is also a map of the soul's final destination.

How we relate: Relate to it by asking what you are clinging to that cannot finally satisfy; the name points beyond short-lived victories.

Story: Bharata's later detachment after the deer episode illustrates how the Lord patiently leads the soul beyond subtle attachments; this name resonates there.

451 sarvadarśī सर्वदर्शी

Meaning: All-seeing.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

452 vimuktātmā विमुक्तात्मा

Meaning: Whose self is ever free.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

453 sarvajñaḥ सर्वज्ञः

Meaning: Omniscient.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

454 jñānam-uttamam ज्ञानमुत्तमम्

Meaning: Supreme knowledge.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

455 suvrataḥ सुव्रतः

Meaning: Of excellent vows.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

456 sumukhaḥ सुमुखः

Meaning: Of gracious face.

Why this name may be here: This name shows that Vishnu's benevolence is not sentimental; it is the steady willingness to receive even the imperfect devotee.

How we relate: Use this name when you feel unworthy; it reminds you that the Lord often responds first to sincerity, not perfection.

Story: Draupadi's appeal is answered not because her situation is elegant but because her surrender is total; this name reveals compassionate response.

457 sūkṣmaḥ सूक्ष्मः

Meaning: Subtle.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

458 sughoṣaḥ सुघोषः

Meaning: Of beautiful sound.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

459 sukhadaḥ सुखदः

Meaning: Giver of happiness.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

460 suhṛt सुहृत्

Meaning: True friend.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes gentle names so that fear of divine grandeur is softened by trust in divine nearness.

How we relate: Use this name when you feel unworthy; it reminds you that the Lord often responds first to sincerity, not perfection.

Story: Gajendra is heard across species and circumstance; the story matches a name that refuses to limit divine care.

461 manoharaḥ मनोहरः

Meaning: Captivating to the mind.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

462 jitakrodhaḥ जितक्रोधः

Meaning: Master of anger.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

463 vīrabāhuḥ वीरबाहुः

Meaning: Strong-armed hero.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

464 vidāraṇaḥ विदारणः

Meaning: The cleaver, destroyer of evil.

Why this name may be here: Names of protection show why devotees trust Vishnu: his greatness is not distant power but power that bends toward preserving dharma.

How we relate: Repeat this name when fear rises, then ask where you can act with courage rather than collapse into helplessness.

Story: Krishna lifts Govardhana to shelter Vraja from Indra's storm; this name belongs to divine strength expressed as refuge.

465 svāpanaḥ स्वापनः

Meaning: He who causes rest and sleep.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

466 svavaśaḥ स्ववशः

Meaning: Under his own control alone.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

467 vyāpī व्यापी

468 naikātmā नैकात्मा

469 naikakarmakṛt नैककर्मकृत्

Meaning: Performer of many acts.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

470 vatsaraḥ वत्सरः

Meaning: The year, the cycle.

Why this name may be here: This epithet reminds the listener that the Lord is not hurried by time because time itself moves within his ordinance.

How we relate: Carry it by living with accountability, because a universe held by order also remembers consequence.

Story: Krishna delays open confrontation with Kamsa until the right moment, showing time used as instrument rather than obstacle.

471 vatsalaḥ वत्सलः

Meaning: Tenderly affectionate.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

472 vatsī वत्सी

Meaning: He who cherishes or owns the calf-like devotees.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

475 dharmagup धर्मगुप्

Meaning: Protector of dharma.

Why this name may be here: Names of protection show why devotees trust Vishnu: his greatness is not distant power but power that bends toward preserving dharma.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: Draupadi's helpless cry draws Krishna's intervention in the assembly; the name suits the Lord who protects dignity when human power fails.

476 dharmakṛt धर्मकृत्

481 akṣaram अक्षरम्

How we relate: Relate to it by asking what you are clinging to that cannot finally satisfy; the name points beyond short-lived victories.

Story: In Vaikuntha descriptions, liberated beings serve without rivalry or lack; this name points to that fulfilled state.

482 avijñātā अविज्ञाता

483 sahasrāṁśuḥ सहस्रांशुः

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

484 vidhātā विधाता

Meaning: The ordainer.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

485 kṛtalakṣaṇaḥ कृतलक्षणः

Meaning: Marked by all auspicious signs.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

486 gabhastinemiḥ गभस्तिनेमिः

Meaning: Hub of the rays of light.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

487 sattvasthaḥ सत्त्वस्थः

Meaning: Established in purity and clarity.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

488 siṁhaḥ सिंहः

Meaning: The lion.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

489 bhūtamaheśvaraḥ भूतमहेश्वरः

Meaning: Great lord of all beings.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

490 ādidevaḥ आदिदेवः

Meaning: The primordial deity.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

491 mahādevaḥ महादेवः

Meaning: The great shining lord.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

492 deveśaḥ देवेशः

Meaning: Lord of the devas.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

493 devabhṛd-guruḥ देवभृद्गुरुः

Meaning: Teacher even of the gods who support heaven.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

494 uttaraḥ उत्तरः

Meaning: The one who carries across, the higher one.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

495 gopatiḥ गोपतिः

Meaning: Lord of cows, earth, and beings.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Krishna's flute summons the hearts of Vraja, showing beauty that governs without coercion.

496 goptā गोप्ता

Meaning: Protector.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes guarding and conquering names to show that compassion in Vishnu is active; it confronts chaos rather than merely overlooking it.

How we relate: Repeat this name when fear rises, then ask where you can act with courage rather than collapse into helplessness.

Story: Gajendra's appeal from the lake brings Vishnu immediately on Garuda; this epithet lives in that swift rescue of the desperate.

497 jñānagamyaḥ ज्ञानगम्यः

Meaning: Attainable through knowledge.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

498 purātanaḥ पुरातनः

Meaning: Ancient and primordial.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

499 śarīrabhūtabhṛt शरीरभूतभृत्

Meaning: Sustainer of embodied beings.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

500 bhoktā भोक्ता

Meaning: The enjoyer.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

Names 501-600

501 kapīndraḥ कपीन्द्रः

Meaning: Lord among beings of strength; also monkey-lord ally.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

502 bhūridakṣiṇaḥ भूरिदक्षिणः

Meaning: Bountiful giver.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

503 somapaḥ सोमपः

Meaning: Drinker of soma.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

504 amṛtapaḥ अमृतपः

Meaning: Drinker of immortality.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

505 somaḥ सोमः

Meaning: The moonlike, soothing one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: This name becomes real when ego yields a little and action is done with sacred intention rather than constant self-display.

Story: As Yajna in primordial times, Vishnu upholds cosmic order through sacrificial harmony; the name fits the deity who is both rite and fruit.

506 purujit पुरुजित्

Meaning: Conqueror of many.

Why this name may be here: Names of protection show why devotees trust Vishnu: his greatness is not distant power but power that bends toward preserving dharma.

How we relate: Repeat this name when fear rises, then ask where you can act with courage rather than collapse into helplessness.

Story: Rama defends the sages of the forest and keeps his father's word despite hardship; this name honors steadfast protective dharma.

507 puruṣattamaḥ पुरुषत्तमः

Meaning: Best of persons.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

508 vinayaḥ विनयः

Meaning: Discipline and humility.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

509 jayaḥ जयः

Meaning: Victory.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes guarding and conquering names to show that compassion in Vishnu is active; it confronts chaos rather than merely overlooking it.

How we relate: Relate to it by becoming dependable for others; to honor the protector is to make your own strength shelter someone weaker.

Story: Vishnu destroys Madhu and Kaitabha when cosmic order is threatened; the story shows protection at the scale of creation itself.

510 satyasaṅdhaḥ सत्यसन्धः

Meaning: True to his promise.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

511 dāśārhaḥ दाशार्हः

Meaning: Of the Yadava line.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

512 sātvataṁ patiḥ सात्वतां पतिः

Meaning: Lord of the Satvatas.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

513 jīvaḥ जीवः

Meaning: Life principle in all.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

514 vinayitāsākṣī विनयितासाक्षी

Meaning: Witness of the disciplined.

Why this name may be here: This name suggests that divine intimacy often appears first as interior steadiness rather than outer spectacle.

How we relate: Relate to it through breath, restraint, silence, and attention; interior order makes devotion less reactive and more stable.

Story: Kapila's instruction to Devahuti joins analysis and devotion, making this name shine as inner clarity.

515 mukundaḥ मुकुन्दः

Meaning: Giver of liberation.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes these names to tell Yudhishthira that refuge in Vishnu is not temporary relief but the road to lasting freedom.

How we relate: Relate to it by asking what you are clinging to that cannot finally satisfy; the name points beyond short-lived victories.

Story: Dhruva seeks a kingdom but receives direct vision and a transformed heart; this name matches the Lord who turns ambition toward liberation.

516 amitavikramaḥ अमितविक्रमः

Meaning: Of boundless stride and valor.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: The reunion with Bharata displays a form of sovereignty anchored in restraint rather than possession.

517 ambhonidhiḥ अम्भोनिधिः

Meaning: Ocean, repository of waters.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: Matsya guides the ark through the deluge, showing the Lord present in waters both destructive and protective.

518 anantātmā अनन्तात्मा

Meaning: Of infinite self.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

519 mahodadhiśayaḥ महोदधिशयः

Meaning: He who reclines on the great ocean.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: This name is lived when gratitude replaces casual consumption and the natural world is treated as sacred participation, not raw material only.

Story: Krishna dances upon Kaliya in the Yamuna, purifying poisoned waters; this name aligns with divine mastery within nature.

520 antakaḥ अन्तकः

Meaning: Ender of all at the right time.

Why this name may be here: This epithet reminds the listener that the Lord is not hurried by time because time itself moves within his ordinance.

How we relate: Relate to it by accepting rhythm: discipline, patience, and timely action are small human reflections of cosmic order.

Story: Bhishma himself waits for the auspicious time of uttarayana before departing, a fitting narrative backdrop for names of divine timing and order.

521 ajaḥ अजः

Meaning: Unborn.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

522 mahārhaḥ महार्हः

Meaning: Supremely worthy of worship.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

523 svābhāvyaḥ स्वाभाव्यः

Meaning: Existing by his own nature.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

524 jitāmitraḥ जितामित्रः

Meaning: Conqueror of foes.

Why this name may be here: This name stresses that divine strength is meaningful because it rescues the vulnerable and re-orders what has gone crooked.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: Krishna breaks Kamsa's reign after years of hidden preparation; this name reflects a protection that can be patient before it becomes dramatic.

525 pramodanaḥ प्रमोदनः

Meaning: Giver of joy.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

526 ānandaḥ आनन्दः

Meaning: Bliss itself.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

527 nandanaḥ नन्दनः

Meaning: The delightful one.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

528 nandaḥ नन्दः

Meaning: Joy.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

529 satyadharmā सत्यधर्मा

Meaning: Whose dharma is true.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

530 trivikramaḥ त्रिविक्रमः

Meaning: He of the three strides.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Bali's surrender of even his own head under Vamana's request reveals the Lord as purifier of generosity.

531 maharṣiḥ kapilācāryaḥ महर्षिः कपिलाचार्यः

Meaning: The great sage Kapila.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

532 kṛtajñaḥ कृतज्ञः

Meaning: Knower of every act and gratitude.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

533 medinīpatiḥ मेदिनीपतिः

Meaning: Lord of the earth.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: Govardhana itself becomes a sign of worship when Krishna turns the community toward the mountain, cows, and rain that sustain life.

534 tripadaḥ त्रिपदः

Meaning: He whose stride is threefold.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

535 tridaśādhyakṣaḥ त्रिदशाध्यक्षः

Meaning: Overseer of the devas.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Akrura's pilgrimage to meet Krishna transforms expectation into revelation; this name fits wisdom that descends as vision, not abstraction alone.

536 mahāśṛṅgaḥ महाशृङ्गः

Meaning: Great-horned; fish or boar-like rescuing form.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

537 kṛtāntakṛt कृतान्तकृत्

Meaning: Maker of the end of time or death.

Why this name may be here: This epithet reminds the listener that the Lord is not hurried by time because time itself moves within his ordinance.

How we relate: Use this name when restless about results; it teaches that maturation has its season and cannot be bullied into ripeness.

Story: Rama's exile ripens many destinies through disciplined endurance; this name suits a Lord whose order unfolds through season and patience.

538 mahāvarāhaḥ महावराहः

Meaning: The great boar incarnation.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Varaha dives into the cosmic waters and raises Bhudevi on his tusks; earth-related names naturally resonate with this act.

539 govindaḥ गोविन्दः

Meaning: Finder and protector of cows, earth, and senses.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: The rescue of the boys from the forest fire reveals quiet divine guardianship amid play.

540 suṣeṇaḥ सुषेणः

Meaning: He of noble armies.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

541 kanakāṅgadī कनकाङ्गदी

Meaning: Adorned with golden armlets.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

542 guhyaḥ गुह्यः

Meaning: Secret, mysterious.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

543 gabhīraḥ गभीरः

Meaning: Deep and profound.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

544 gahanaḥ गहनः

Meaning: Hard to fathom.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

545 guptaḥ गुप्तः

Meaning: Hidden, protected.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

546 cakragadādharaḥ चक्रगदाधरः

Meaning: Bearer of discus and mace.

Why this name may be here: Weapon names are included not to glorify violence but to symbolize precise divine intervention against adharma and inner disorder.

How we relate: Relate to it by letting discernment cut illusion, letting discipline strike laziness, and letting courage break harmful habit.

Story: The Sharnga bow in many Purana narratives symbolizes readiness to protect without hesitation; this name extends that image.

547 vedhāḥ वेधाः

Meaning: Creator.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

548 svāṅgaḥ स्वाङ्गः

Meaning: Whose limbs are perfect and self-sufficient.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

549 ajitaḥ अजितः

Meaning: Unconquered.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

550 kṛṣṇaḥ कृष्णः

Meaning: The dark and all-attractive one.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

551 dṛḍhaḥ दृढः

Meaning: Firm and steadfast.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

552 saṅkarṣaṇo'cyutaḥ सङ्कर्षणोऽच्युतः

Meaning: Sankarshana, the infallible one.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

553 varuṇaḥ वरुणः

Meaning: Lord of waters and moral depth.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: The descent of the Ganga through divine mediation reflects a cosmos where sacred waters carry grace into the world.

554 vāruṇaḥ वारुणः

Meaning: Related to the watery power.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: The serpent Ananta becomes Vishnu's couch, revealing even immense natural force as restful service in the divine presence.

555 vṛkṣaḥ वृक्षः

Meaning: The tree, the support of life.

Why this name may be here: Elemental names reveal that the sacred is not outside creation; mountains, waters, light, and life-currents all mirror Vishnu's presence.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: Garuda's service shows sky, speed, and bird-power offered wholly to the Lord; this name resonates with that elevation.

556 puṣkarākṣaḥ पुष्कराक्षः

Meaning: Lotus-eyed.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: Carry it by making your surroundings and conduct worthy of the presence of Sri.

Story: In Tirumala lore, Srinivasa receives and protects the wealth of devotion offered by countless pilgrims; such narratives illuminate auspicious names.

557 mahāmanāḥ महामनाः

Meaning: Great-minded.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

558 bhagavān भगवान्

Meaning: The possessor of all divine excellences.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

559 bhagahā भगहा

Meaning: Destroyer of false prosperity.

Why this name may be here: This name stresses that divine strength is meaningful because it rescues the vulnerable and re-orders what has gone crooked.

How we relate: Relate to it by becoming dependable for others; to honor the protector is to make your own strength shelter someone weaker.

Story: When Banasura's arrogance overwhelms his own good fortune, Krishna defeats him without needless cruelty; this name shows protective justice with restraint.

560 ānandī आनन्दी

Meaning: Joyful and joy-giving.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

561 vanamālī वनमाली

Meaning: Wearer of the forest garland.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

562 halāyudhaḥ हलायुधः

Meaning: Wielder of the plough.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Balarama's plough-bearing strength symbolizes grounded power that prepares and protects; names linked to Halayudha fit this steady might.

563 ādityaḥ आदित्यः

Meaning: Son of Aditi, the radiant one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

564 jyotirādityaḥ ज्योतिरादित्यः

Meaning: Solar splendor of light.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

565 sahiṣṇuḥ सहिष्णुः

Meaning: Forbearing.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

566 gatisattamaḥ गतिसत्तमः

Meaning: The highest goal and movement.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shifts attention from worldly success to the deeper question of what finally releases sorrow.

How we relate: Carry it by practicing detachment with love, not indifference.

Story: Gajendra's rescue is not merely escape from a crocodile but deliverance from bondage and forgetfulness; this epithet fits that deeper release.

567 sudhanvā सुधन्वा

Meaning: Of excellent bow.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma closes and intensifies the hymn with martial imagery because Vishnu's compassion sometimes arrives as sharp correction.

How we relate: Carry it by becoming precise in action rather than aggressive in ego.

Story: Panchajanya, Krishna's conch, announces righteous action and awakens hearts before battle; the name reflects sound as sacred power.

568 khaṇḍaparaśuḥ खण्डपरशुः

Meaning: Wielder of the battle-axe.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

569 dāruṇaḥ दारुणः

Meaning: Stern and formidable to evil.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

570 draviṇapradaḥ द्रविणप्रदः

Meaning: Giver of wealth and resources.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

571 divaḥspṛk दिवःस्पृक्

Meaning: Touching the heavens.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.
