How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

572 sarvadṛgvyāsaḥ सर्वदृग्व्यासः

Meaning: The all-seeing arranger of knowledge.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Sanatkumara and the sages seek the supreme truth and find it centered in Narayana; the name suits the Lord who is both teacher and taught-of.

573 vācaspatirayonijaḥ वाचस्पतिरयोनिजः

Meaning: Lord of speech, not born from a womb.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: In the Gajendra episode, the elephant's past-life memory of prayer returns at the crucial moment; this name reflects divine knowledge awakening dormant wisdom.

574 trisāmā त्रिसामा

Meaning: Essence of the three Sama chants.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

575 sāmagaḥ सामगः

Meaning: Singer of the Sama.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

576 sāma साम

Meaning: The Sama Veda itself, harmony.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: Krishna instructs Arjuna on the battlefield, turning despair into discernment; this name belongs to the Lord who teaches inside crisis.

577 nirvāṇam निर्वाणम्

Meaning: Supreme peace and release.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shifts attention from worldly success to the deeper question of what finally releases sorrow.

How we relate: Relate to it by asking what you are clinging to that cannot finally satisfy; the name points beyond short-lived victories.

Story: Ajamila's last-minute remembrance opens a path beyond the mechanics of punishment; this name points to grace beyond despair.

578 bheṣajam भेषजम्

Meaning: The medicine.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

579 bhiṣak भिषक्

Meaning: The healer.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

580 saṁnyāsakṛt संन्यासकृत्

Meaning: Giver or institutor of renunciation.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

581 śamaḥ शमः

Meaning: Inner quietude.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

582 śāntaḥ शान्तः

Meaning: Peaceful.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

583 niṣṭhā निष्ठा

Meaning: Steady foundation.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

584 śāntiḥ शान्तिः

Meaning: Peace itself.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

585 parāyaṇam परायणम्

Meaning: The supreme refuge.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

586 śubhāṅgaḥ शुभाङ्गः

Meaning: Of auspicious limbs.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: Rukmini's heart turns unwaveringly toward Krishna before their marriage; this name reflects divine beauty that draws love by its own worth.

587 śāntidaḥ शान्तिदः

Meaning: Giver of peace.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

588 sraṣṭā स्रष्टा

Meaning: Creator.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

589 kumudaḥ कुमुदः

Meaning: Delighter of the earth.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: The moonlike cooling of Krishna's presence in Vraja often follows scenes of heat and turmoil; such names fit the soothing aspect of divinity in nature.

590 kuvaleśayaḥ कुवलेशयः

Meaning: He who rests on the waters or within the lotus-world.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: When Sudama visits Krishna in poverty and returns to quiet abundance, the story shows auspicious grace arriving without humiliation.

591 gohitaḥ गोहितः

Meaning: Well-wisher of cows, earth, and beings.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: This name is lived when gratitude replaces casual consumption and the natural world is treated as sacred participation, not raw material only.

Story: In the forest episodes of the Ramayana, trees, rivers, and mountains become witnesses to Rama's dharma; this name turns the landscape into theology.

592 gopatiḥ गोपतिः

Meaning: Lord of cows and protectees.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Brahma's bewilderment in Vraja shows Krishna's supremacy even in apparently ordinary village life.

593 goptā गोप्ता

Meaning: Protector.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes guarding and conquering names to show that compassion in Vishnu is active; it confronts chaos rather than merely overlooking it.

How we relate: Relate to it by becoming dependable for others; to honor the protector is to make your own strength shelter someone weaker.

Story: In saving the elephant king, Vishnu accepts no delay because the cry is sincere; that immediacy illustrates this name.

594 vṛṣabhākṣaḥ वृषभाक्षः

Meaning: Bull-eyed, or whose vision is dharma.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

595 vṛṣapriyaḥ वृषप्रियः

Meaning: Lover of righteousness.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

596 anivartī अनिवर्ती

Meaning: Never retreating.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

597 nivṛttātmā निवृत्तात्मा

Meaning: Whose self is detached from worldly compulsion.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

598 saṁkṣeptā संक्षेप्ता

Meaning: He who contracts the universe.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

599 kṣemakṛt क्षेमकृत्

Meaning: Maker of welfare and security.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

600 śivaḥ शिवः

Meaning: Auspicious and pure.

Why this name may be here: This epithet teaches that prosperity in its pure sense means a life aligned with sacred order, not mere accumulation.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: The Lord seated with Sri on his chest symbolizes a kingship inseparable from compassion and harmony; this name points to that inseparable splendor.

Names 601-700

601 śrīvatsavakṣāḥ श्रीवत्सवक्षाः

Meaning: He whose chest bears the Śrīvatsa mark.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: Tulasi's devotion becomes eternally joined to Vishnu's worship, showing that auspiciousness includes purity of offering and love.

602 śrīvāsaḥ श्रीवासः

Meaning: Abode of Sri.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: The Samudra Manthana produces many treasures, yet Lakshmi's choosing of Vishnu reveals where true prosperity ultimately rests.

603 śrīpatiḥ श्रीपतिः

Meaning: Lord of Sri.

Why this name may be here: This epithet teaches that prosperity in its pure sense means a life aligned with sacred order, not mere accumulation.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: In the story of Andal's longing for Ranganatha, divine beauty becomes an object of total surrender; this name resonates with that magnetic grace.

604 śrīmatāṁ varaḥ श्रीमतां वरः

Meaning: Best among the glorious.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

605 śrīdaḥ श्रीदः

Meaning: Giver of prosperity and grace.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: In Tirumala lore, Srinivasa receives and protects the wealth of devotion offered by countless pilgrims; such narratives illuminate auspicious names.

606 śrīśaḥ श्रीशः

Meaning: Lord of Sri.

Why this name may be here: This epithet teaches that prosperity in its pure sense means a life aligned with sacred order, not mere accumulation.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: Rukmini's heart turns unwaveringly toward Krishna before their marriage; this name reflects divine beauty that draws love by its own worth.

607 śrīnivāsaḥ श्रीनिवासः

Meaning: Abode of Sri.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: When Sudama visits Krishna in poverty and returns to quiet abundance, the story shows auspicious grace arriving without humiliation.

608 śrīnidhiḥ श्रीनिधिः

Meaning: Treasure of Sri.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: The Lord seated with Sri on his chest symbolizes a kingship inseparable from compassion and harmony; this name points to that inseparable splendor.

609 śrīvibhāvanaḥ श्रीविभावनः

Meaning: Manifestor of auspicious prosperity.

Why this name may be here: This epithet teaches that prosperity in its pure sense means a life aligned with sacred order, not mere accumulation.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: Tulasi's devotion becomes eternally joined to Vishnu's worship, showing that auspiciousness includes purity of offering and love.

610 śrīdharaḥ श्रीधरः

Meaning: Bearer of Sri.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: The Samudra Manthana produces many treasures, yet Lakshmi's choosing of Vishnu reveals where true prosperity ultimately rests.

611 śrīkaraḥ श्रीकरः

Meaning: Maker of auspiciousness.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: This name asks you to redefine wealth as inner richness plus responsible stewardship of what has been entrusted to you.

Story: In the story of Andal's longing for Ranganatha, divine beauty becomes an object of total surrender; this name resonates with that magnetic grace.

612 śreyaḥ श्रेयः

Meaning: The highest good.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

613 śrīmān श्रीमान्

Meaning: Ever endowed with Sri.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: Carry it by making your surroundings and conduct worthy of the presence of Sri.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

614 lokatrayāśrayaḥ लोकत्रयाश्रयः

Meaning: Refuge of the three worlds.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

615 svakṣaḥ स्वक्षः

Meaning: Of beautiful eyes.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

616 svaṅgaḥ स्वङ्गः

Meaning: Of perfect limbs.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

617 śatānandaḥ शतानन्दः

Meaning: Of hundredfold joy.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

618 nandiḥ नन्दिः

Meaning: The rejoicing one.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

619 jyotirgaṇeśvaraḥ ज्योतिर्गणेश्वरः

Meaning: Lord of all luminaries.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

620 vijitātmā विजितात्मा

Meaning: Master of himself.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

621 vidheyātmā विधेयात्मा

Meaning: Whose self is obedient to truth, or who makes beings disciplined.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: As Hayagriva, Vishnu restores the Vedas after they are stolen; the story suits names tied to knowledge and sacred speech.

622 satkīrtiḥ सत्कीर्तिः

Meaning: Of noble fame.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

623 chinnasaṁśayaḥ छिन्नसंशयः

Meaning: Cutter of doubt.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

624 udīrṇaḥ उदीर्णः

Meaning: Lofty and exalted.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

625 sarvataścakṣuḥ सर्वतश्चक्षुः

Meaning: Eyes on every side.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

626 anīśaḥ अनीशः

Meaning: None rules over him.

Why this name may be here: This epithet reminds the listener that the Lord is not hurried by time because time itself moves within his ordinance.

How we relate: Carry it by living with accountability, because a universe held by order also remembers consequence.

Story: Vamana's measured three steps transform cosmic space into sacred order; this epithet reflects the Lord as measurer and ordainer.

627 śāśvatasthiraḥ शाश्वतस्थिरः

Meaning: Eternally stable.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

628 bhūśayaḥ भूशयः

Meaning: He who reclines on the earth or in the hearts of beings.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: This name is lived when gratitude replaces casual consumption and the natural world is treated as sacred participation, not raw material only.

Story: Varaha lifting the earth from cosmic waters makes elemental imagery literal; this name suits Vishnu as guardian of the world-body.

629 bhūṣaṇaḥ भूषणः

Meaning: Ornament of the world.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

630 bhūtiḥ भूतिः

Meaning: Prosperity and glory.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

631 viśokaḥ विशोकः

Meaning: Free from sorrow.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

632 śokanāśanaḥ शोकनाशनः

Meaning: Destroyer of sorrow.

Why this name may be here: This name stresses that divine strength is meaningful because it rescues the vulnerable and re-orders what has gone crooked.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: The Sudarshana protects Ambarisha from Durvasa's fury, proving this name through preservation of the humble devotee.

633 arciṣmān अर्चिष्मान्

Meaning: Effulgent with flames and rays.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

634 arcitaḥ अर्चितः

Meaning: Worshipped.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

635 kumbhaḥ कुम्भः

Meaning: The vessel, fullness.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

636 viśuddhātmā विशुद्धात्मा

Meaning: Of utterly pure self.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

637 viśodhanaḥ विशोधनः

Meaning: The purifier.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

638 aniruddhaḥ अनिरुद्धः

Meaning: Unobstructed; the emanation Aniruddha.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

639 apratirathaḥ अप्रतिरथः

Meaning: Without an equal opponent in battle.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

640 pradyumnaḥ प्रद्युम्नः

Meaning: Splendidly luminous; the emanation Pradyumna.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

641 amitavikramaḥ अमितविक्रमः

Meaning: Of immeasurable valor.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Rama's exile keeps truth to his father's word above comfort, making righteous names shine with moral gravity.

642 kālaneminihā कालनेमिनिहा

Meaning: Slayer of Kālanemi.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

643 vīraḥ वीरः

Meaning: The hero.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

644 śauriḥ शौरिः

Meaning: The heroic one, of the Śūra line.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

645 śūrajaneśvaraḥ शूरजनेश्वरः

Meaning: Lord of the heroic people.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

646 trilokātmā त्रिलोकात्मा

Meaning: Soul of the three worlds.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

647 trilokeśaḥ त्रिलोकेशः

Meaning: Lord of the three worlds.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

648 keśavaḥ केशवः

Meaning: Beautiful-haired one; slayer of Keshi.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: Kurma supports Mount Mandara during the churning, turning mountain and ocean alike into instruments of divine purpose.

649 keśihā केशिहा

Meaning: Slayer of the Keshi demon.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

650 hariḥ हरिः

Meaning: He who removes bondage and sin.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

651 kāmadevaḥ कामदेवः

Meaning: Lord of love.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

652 kāmapālaḥ कामपालः

Meaning: Protector of rightful desire.

Why this name may be here: This name stresses that divine strength is meaningful because it rescues the vulnerable and re-orders what has gone crooked.

How we relate: Relate to it by becoming dependable for others; to honor the protector is to make your own strength shelter someone weaker.

Story: Krishna safeguards Parikshit in the womb from Ashvatthama's weapon; the name fits that unseen guardianship of future dharma.

653 kāmī कामी

Meaning: He who is full in himself yet grants loving fulfilment.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

654 kāntaḥ कान्तः

Meaning: The beloved and beautiful one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

655 kṛtāgamaḥ कृतागमः

Meaning: Source of the sacred traditions.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

656 anirdeśyavapuḥ अनिर्देश्यवपुः

Meaning: Of indescribable form.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

657 viṣṇuḥ विष्णुः

Meaning: The all-pervading one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

658 vīraḥ वीरः

Meaning: The hero.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

659 anantaḥ अनन्तः

Meaning: Endless, infinite.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

660 dhanañjayaḥ धनञ्जयः

Meaning: Winner of wealth and victory.

Why this name may be here: This name stresses that divine strength is meaningful because it rescues the vulnerable and re-orders what has gone crooked.

How we relate: Repeat this name when fear rises, then ask where you can act with courage rather than collapse into helplessness.

Story: As Mohini, Vishnu protects the devas by recovering the amrita from a chaotic scramble; this name reflects strategic preservation.

661 brahmaṇyaḥ ब्रह्मण्यः

Meaning: Protector of sacred wisdom and the brahmanical order.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: Kapila teaches Devahuti the way of liberation and right seeing; this name resonates with Vishnu as clarifying intelligence.

662 brahmakṛt ब्रह्मकृत्

Meaning: Maker of spiritual knowledge and sacred order.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: This name becomes real when ego yields a little and action is done with sacred intention rather than constant self-display.

Story: The Rajasuya of Yudhishthira culminates in Krishna receiving first honors, showing that all offerings find their center in him.

663 brahmā ब्रह्मा

Meaning: The creator principle.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

664 brahma ब्रह्म

Meaning: The absolute reality.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

665 brahmavivardhanaḥ ब्रह्मविवर्धनः

Meaning: Promoter of spiritual wisdom.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: Daksha's disrupted sacrifice is restored only when the deeper principle of divine order is honored; this name suits Vishnu as the true sanctifier of ritual.

666 brahmavit ब्रह्मवित्

Meaning: Knower of Brahman.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

667 brāhmaṇaḥ ब्राह्मणः

Meaning: He who is steeped in sacred wisdom.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

668 brahmī ब्रह्मी

Meaning: Embodied in Brahmic wisdom.

Why this name may be here: The hymn uses such names to join devotion with clarity: the Lord illumines scripture, conscience, and right discernment together.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

669 brahmajñaḥ ब्रह्मज्ञः

Meaning: Knower of the absolute.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

670 brāhmaṇapriyaḥ ब्राह्मणप्रियः

Meaning: Dear to seekers of sacred knowledge.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: Akrura's pilgrimage to meet Krishna transforms expectation into revelation; this name fits wisdom that descends as vision, not abstraction alone.

671 mahākramaḥ महाक्रमः

Meaning: Of great stride.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: The bridge to Lanka shows leadership that gathers many beings into one dharmic purpose.

672 mahākarmā महाकर्मा

Meaning: Doer of great acts.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

673 mahātejāḥ महातेजाः

Meaning: Of great splendor.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

674 mahoragaḥ महोरगः

Meaning: Great serpent-like cosmic power.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: Matsya guides the ark through the deluge, showing the Lord present in waters both destructive and protective.

675 mahākratuḥ महाक्रतुः

Meaning: The great sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: When Krishna teaches that offerings made with devotion are accepted even if simple, this epithet becomes inward and universal.

676 mahāyajvā महायज्वा

Meaning: Great performer of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Such names connect temple, fire, mantra, and surrender into a single vision centered on Vishnu.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: Bali's gift to Vamana turns a ritual act into total surrender; this name fits the Lord who receives both offering and offerer.

677 mahāyajñaḥ महायज्ञः

Meaning: The great sacrifice itself.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: The sages at Naimisharanya keep sacrificial time alive through remembrance of Narayana; the name belongs to the divine heart of ritual continuity.

678 mahāhaviḥ महाहविः

Meaning: The great oblation.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: In Ambarisha's observance of Ekadashi and Dvadashi, discipline itself becomes worship; this name suits Vishnu as the fulfiller of vowed offering.

679 stavyaḥ स्तव्यः

Meaning: Worthy of praise.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

680 stavapriyaḥ स्तवप्रियः

Meaning: Lover of praise.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

681 stotram स्तोत्रम्

Meaning: The hymn itself.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

682 stutiḥ स्तुतिः

Meaning: Praise.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

683 stotā स्तोता

Meaning: The praiser.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

684 raṇapriyaḥ रणप्रियः

Meaning: Lover of righteous battle.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

685 pūrṇaḥ पूर्णः

Meaning: He who is complete and perfect.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

686 pūrayitā पूरयिता

Meaning: The fulfiller.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

687 puṇyaḥ पुण्यः

Meaning: Holy and meritorious.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

688 puṇyakīrtiḥ पुण्यकीर्तिः

Meaning: Of sacred fame.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

689 anāmayaḥ अनामयः

Meaning: Free from affliction.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

690 manojavaḥ मनोजवः

Meaning: Swift as the mind.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

691 tīrthakaraḥ तीर्थकरः

Meaning: Maker of sacred crossings.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

692 vasuretāḥ वसुरेताः

Meaning: Whose seed is abundance and light.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

693 vasupradaḥ वसुप्रदः

Meaning: Giver of wealth.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

694 vasupradaḥ वसुप्रदः

Meaning: Bestower again of treasure.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

695 vāsudevaḥ वासुदेवः

Meaning: The indwelling Lord in all; son of Vasudeva.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

696 vasuḥ वसुः

Meaning: The indwelling treasure.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

697 vasumanāḥ वसुमनाः

Meaning: Of generous and noble mind.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

698 haviḥ हविः

Meaning: The oblation.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: This name becomes real when ego yields a little and action is done with sacred intention rather than constant self-display.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

699 sadgatiḥ सद्गतिः

Meaning: The good goal.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shifts attention from worldly success to the deeper question of what finally releases sorrow.

How we relate: Use this name in moments of grief or exhaustion; it turns the heart toward the freedom that survives change.

Story: The Four Kumaras attain devotion through the fragrance of Tulasi at Vishnu's feet, showing liberation softened into loving nearness.

700 satkṛtiḥ सत्कृतिः

Meaning: Of noble action and honor.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

701 sattā सत्ता

Meaning: Pure being.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

702 sadbhūtiḥ सद्भूतिः

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

703 satparāyaṇaḥ सत्परायणः

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

704 śūrasenaḥ शूरसेनः

Meaning: Of the Sura or Yadu line.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

705 yaduśreṣṭhaḥ यदुश्रेष्ठः

Meaning: Best of the Yadus.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

706 sannivāsaḥ सन्निवासः

Meaning: Abode of the noble.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

707 suyāmunaḥ सुयामुनः

Meaning: Blessed by the Yamuna region.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

708 bhūtāvāsaḥ भूतावासः

Meaning: Abode of all beings.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

709 vāsudevaḥ वासुदेवः

Meaning: The indwelling Lord in all.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

710 sarvāsunilayaḥ सर्वासुनिलयः

Meaning: Abode of all life-breaths.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: Krishna dances upon Kaliya in the Yamuna, purifying poisoned waters; this name aligns with divine mastery within nature.

711 analaḥ अनलः

Meaning: Fire, endless energy.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: Govardhana itself becomes a sign of worship when Krishna turns the community toward the mountain, cows, and rain that sustain life.

712 darpahā दर्पहा

Meaning: Destroyer of pride.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes guarding and conquering names to show that compassion in Vishnu is active; it confronts chaos rather than merely overlooking it.

How we relate: Repeat this name when fear rises, then ask where you can act with courage rather than collapse into helplessness.

Story: Narasimha bursts forth to save Prahlada and break tyrannical pride; this name fits the Lord whose protection arrives exactly where hope seems blocked.

713 darpadaḥ दर्पदः

Meaning: Giver of healthy self-respect and power.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

714 dṛptaḥ दृप्तः

Meaning: Majestic and confident.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

715 durdharaḥ दुर्धरः

Meaning: Difficult to hold or restrain.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

716 athāparājitaḥ अथापराजितः

Meaning: The unsurpassed, never defeated one.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

717 viśvamūrtiḥ विश्वमूर्तिः

Meaning: Whose form is the whole universe.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

718 mahāmūrtiḥ महामूर्तिः

Meaning: Of immense form.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

719 dīptamūrtiḥ दीप्तमूर्तिः

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

720 amūrtimān अमूर्तिमान्

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

721 anekamūrtiḥ अनेकमूर्तिः

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

722 avyaktaḥ अव्यक्तः

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

723 śatamūrtiḥ शतमूर्तिः

724 śatānanaḥ शताननः

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.
