843 svadhṛtaḥ स्वधृतः

Meaning: Self-supported.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

844 svāsyaḥ स्वास्यः

Meaning: Of beautiful face or noble speech.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Use this name when confused; it encourages a mind that asks what is true, not merely what is convenient.

Story: As Hayagriva, Vishnu restores the Vedas after they are stolen; the story suits names tied to knowledge and sacred speech.

845 prāgvaṁśaḥ प्राग्वंशः

Meaning: Ancient lineage, primordial source.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

846 vaṁśavardhanaḥ वंशवर्धनः

Meaning: One who expands the lineage of dharma.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

847 bhārabhṛt भारभृत्

Meaning: Bearer of burdens.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

848 kathitaḥ कथितः

Meaning: Well proclaimed in scripture.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

849 yogī योगी

Meaning: The yogin.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

850 yogīśaḥ योगीशः

Meaning: Lord of yogins.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

851 sarvakāmadaḥ सर्वकामदः

Meaning: Giver of all worthy aims.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

852 āśramaḥ आश्रमः

Meaning: Resting place and stage of discipline.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: The defeat of Ravana manifests justice that answers long-standing misuse of power.

853 śramaṇaḥ श्रमणः

Meaning: He who labors for the good, ascetic one.

Why this name may be here: Interior names show Vishnu as the still center discovered in discipline, attention, and inward purification.

How we relate: It becomes practical when you choose pause over impulse and remembrance over distraction.

Story: Prahlada remembers Hari continuously even under torture; this name reflects yoga as unbroken recollection.

854 kṣāmaḥ क्षामः

Meaning: Lean through austerity.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

855 suparṇaḥ सुपर्णः

Meaning: Beautiful-winged, Garuda-like.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

856 vāyuvāhanaḥ वायुवाहनः

Meaning: Driver of the wind.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: This name is lived when gratitude replaces casual consumption and the natural world is treated as sacred participation, not raw material only.

Story: The descent of the Ganga through divine mediation reflects a cosmos where sacred waters carry grace into the world.

857 dhanurdharaḥ धनुर्धरः

Meaning: Wielder of the bow.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma closes and intensifies the hymn with martial imagery because Vishnu's compassion sometimes arrives as sharp correction.

How we relate: Relate to it by letting discernment cut illusion, letting discipline strike laziness, and letting courage break harmful habit.

Story: Kaumodaki, the mace, stands for solid strength in defense of order; such weapon names remind us that dharma needs force as well as ideals.

858 dhanurvedaḥ धनुर्वेदः

Meaning: Knower of archery and martial wisdom.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: Kapila teaches Devahuti the way of liberation and right seeing; this name resonates with Vishnu as clarifying intelligence.

859 daṇḍaḥ दण्डः

Meaning: The rod of discipline.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

860 damayitā दमयिता

Meaning: The tamer.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

861 damaḥ दमः

Meaning: Self-control.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

862 aparājitaḥ अपराजितः

Meaning: Unconquered.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

863 sarvasahaḥ सर्वसहः

Meaning: All-enduring.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

864 niyantā नियन्ता

Meaning: The controller.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

865 aniyamaḥ अनियमः

Meaning: Beyond all limiting rules.

Why this name may be here: Time-bearing names show that Vishnu is praised as the measure behind change, decay, renewal, and moral consequence.

How we relate: Carry it by living with accountability, because a universe held by order also remembers consequence.

Story: The return of the monsoon after Govardhana's lesson reminds Indra that power too has a rightful rhythm under higher law.

866 ayamaḥ अयमः

Meaning: Not confined by restraint or death.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

867 sattvavān सत्त्ववान्

Meaning: Rich in purity and courage.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

868 sāttvikaḥ सात्त्विकः

Meaning: Pure in essence.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

869 satyaḥ सत्यः

Meaning: Truth itself.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

870 satyadharmaparāyaṇaḥ सत्यधर्मपरायणः

Meaning: Wholly devoted to truth and dharma.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Carry this name through study, reflection, and honest self-examination; it invites intelligence to become a form of worship.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

871 abhiprāyaḥ अभिप्रायः

Meaning: Inner intent, divine purpose.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

872 priyārhaḥ प्रियार्हः

Meaning: Worthy of love.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

873 arhaḥ अर्हः

Meaning: Worthy of worship.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

874 priyakṛt प्रियकृत्

Meaning: Doer of what is dear and beneficial.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

875 prītivardhanaḥ प्रीतिवर्धनः

Meaning: Increaser of love.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

876 vihāyasagatiḥ विहायसगतिः

Meaning: Moving through the sky.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: The serpent Ananta becomes Vishnu's couch, revealing even immense natural force as restful service in the divine presence.

877 jyotiḥ ज्योतिः

Meaning: Light itself.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

878 suruciḥ सुरुचिः

Meaning: Of lovely brilliance.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

879 hutabhuk हुतभुक्

Meaning: Consumer of the oblation.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: Daksha's disrupted sacrifice is restored only when the deeper principle of divine order is honored; this name suits Vishnu as the true sanctifier of ritual.

880 vibhuḥ विभुः

Meaning: The all-pervading mighty one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

881 raviḥ रविः

Meaning: The sun.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: Garuda's service shows sky, speed, and bird-power offered wholly to the Lord; this name resonates with that elevation.

882 virocanaḥ विरोचनः

Meaning: The shining one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

883 sūryaḥ सूर्यः

Meaning: Solar radiance.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

884 savitā सविता

Meaning: The impeller, the vivifier.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

885 ravilocanaḥ रविलोचनः

Meaning: Whose eye is the sun.

Why this name may be here: Elemental names reveal that the sacred is not outside creation; mountains, waters, light, and life-currents all mirror Vishnu's presence.

How we relate: Relate to it by paying attention to sky, light, rivers, food, and breath; creation becomes a classroom of divine hints.

Story: The moonlike cooling of Krishna's presence in Vraja often follows scenes of heat and turmoil; such names fit the soothing aspect of divinity in nature.

886 anantaḥ अनन्तः

Meaning: Endless.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

887 hutabhuk हुतभुक्

Meaning: Receiver of sacred offering.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Relate to it by turning daily effort into offering: food, speech, labor, and restraint can all be placed inwardly before the Lord.

Story: When Krishna teaches that offerings made with devotion are accepted even if simple, this epithet becomes inward and universal.

888 bhoktā भोक्ता

Meaning: Enjoyer.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

889 sukhadaḥ सुखदः

Meaning: Giver of happiness.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

890 naikajaḥ नैकजः

Meaning: Born in many ways, manifest in many forms.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

891 agrajaḥ अग्रजः

Meaning: The first-born, the one before all.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

892 anirviṇṇaḥ अनिर्विण्णः

Meaning: Never despondent.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

893 sadāmarṣī सदामर्षी

Meaning: Ever patient.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

894 lokādhiṣṭhānam लोकाधिष्ठानम्

Meaning: Foundation of the worlds.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

895 adbhutaḥ अद्भुतः

Meaning: Wonderful and wondrous.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

896 sanāt सनात्

Meaning: Ancient, eternal.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

897 sanātanatamaḥ सनातनतमः

Meaning: The most eternal.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

898 kapilaḥ कपिलः

Meaning: Tawny-hued; also sage Kapila.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

899 kapiḥ कपिः

Meaning: The radiant one, or the boar/monkey-linked form.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

900 avyayaḥ अव्ययः

Meaning: Imperishable.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes these names to tell Yudhishthira that refuge in Vishnu is not temporary relief but the road to lasting freedom.

How we relate: Carry it by practicing detachment with love, not indifference.

Story: Uddhava is guided beyond grief by Krishna's final teachings; the name suits liberation through understanding and surrender.

Names 901-1000

901 svastidaḥ स्वस्तिदः

Meaning: Giver of well-being.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

902 svastikṛt स्वस्तिकृत्

Meaning: Maker of auspiciousness.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: Relate to it by caring for beauty, cleanliness, gratitude, and generosity; auspiciousness grows where life is ordered and respectful.

Story: When Sudama visits Krishna in poverty and returns to quiet abundance, the story shows auspicious grace arriving without humiliation.

903 svasti स्वस्ति

Meaning: Auspiciousness itself.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: Relate to it by caring for beauty, cleanliness, gratitude, and generosity; auspiciousness grows where life is ordered and respectful.

Story: The Lord seated with Sri on his chest symbolizes a kingship inseparable from compassion and harmony; this name points to that inseparable splendor.

904 svastibhuk स्वस्तिभुक्

Meaning: Enjoyer of auspicious offerings.

Why this name may be here: This epithet teaches that prosperity in its pure sense means a life aligned with sacred order, not mere accumulation.

How we relate: Relate to it by caring for beauty, cleanliness, gratitude, and generosity; auspiciousness grows where life is ordered and respectful.

Story: Tulasi's devotion becomes eternally joined to Vishnu's worship, showing that auspiciousness includes purity of offering and love.

905 svastidakṣiṇaḥ स्वस्तिदक्षिणः

Meaning: Giver of auspicious rewards.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma places these names so the devotee remembers that divine sovereignty is accompanied by harmony, radiance, and the presence of Sri.

How we relate: Relate to it by caring for beauty, cleanliness, gratitude, and generosity; auspiciousness grows where life is ordered and respectful.

Story: The Samudra Manthana produces many treasures, yet Lakshmi's choosing of Vishnu reveals where true prosperity ultimately rests.

906 araudraḥ अरौद्रः

Meaning: Not cruel, free from wrath toward devotees.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

907 kuṇḍalī कुंडली

Meaning: Wearer of earrings; coiled power.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

908 cakrī चक्री

Meaning: Bearer of the discus.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shows that the Lord's instruments are extensions of protection, justice, and awakened order.

How we relate: Carry it by becoming precise in action rather than aggressive in ego.

Story: Nandaka, the sword of wisdom, is often read as cutting ignorance; this name suits divine intervention that clarifies as it conquers.

909 vikramī विक्रमी

Meaning: Heroic in stride.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

910 ūrjitaśāsanaḥ ऊर्जितशासनः

Meaning: Of powerful command.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

911 śabdātigaḥ शब्दातिगः

Meaning: Beyond sound and verbal definition.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

912 śabdasahaḥ शब्दसहः

Meaning: Patient with sound; supporter of sacred word.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

913 śiśiraḥ शिशिरः

Meaning: Cooling and soothing.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

914 śarvarīkaraḥ शर्वरीकरः

Meaning: Maker of the night or rest-giver.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

915 akrūraḥ अक्रूरः

Meaning: Not cruel; also linked with Akrura.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

916 peśalaḥ पेशलः

Meaning: Graceful and refined.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

917 dakṣaḥ दक्षः

Meaning: Skillful.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

918 dakṣiṇaḥ दक्षिणः

Meaning: Gentle, skillful, and giving.

Why this name may be here: Names of kindness keep the hymn intimate: the all-pervading Lord is also approachable, patient, and responsive to the heart.

How we relate: Relate to it by softening judgment without lowering standards; true kindness combines warmth with steadiness.

Story: Sabari's patient devotion is fulfilled when Rama accepts her offering without contempt; the name reflects divine gentleness toward sincerity.

919 kṣamiṇāṁ varaḥ क्षमिणां वरः

Meaning: Best among the forgiving.

Why this name may be here: This name shows that Vishnu's benevolence is not sentimental; it is the steady willingness to receive even the imperfect devotee.

How we relate: Relate to it by softening judgment without lowering standards; true kindness combines warmth with steadiness.

Story: Ajamila is rescued by the power of Narayana's name even after a fallen life; this epithet shows mercy reaching where merit cannot.

920 vidvattamaḥ विद्वत्तमः

Meaning: Greatest among the wise.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

921 vītabhayaḥ वीतभयः

Meaning: Free from fear and remover of fear.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

922 puṇyaśravaṇakīrtanaḥ पुण्यश्रवणकीर्तनः

Meaning: Whose hearing and chanting are meritorious.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

923 uttāraṇaḥ उत्तारणः

Meaning: He who lifts across.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

924 duṣkṛtihā दुष्कृतिहा

Meaning: Destroyer of misdeeds.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes guarding and conquering names to show that compassion in Vishnu is active; it confronts chaos rather than merely overlooking it.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: When Banasura's arrogance overwhelms his own good fortune, Krishna defeats him without needless cruelty; this name shows protective justice with restraint.

925 puṇyaḥ पुण्यः

Meaning: Holy one.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

926 duḥsvapnanāśanaḥ दुःस्वप्ननाशनः

Meaning: Destroyer of bad dreams.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes guarding and conquering names to show that compassion in Vishnu is active; it confronts chaos rather than merely overlooking it.

How we relate: Relate to it by becoming dependable for others; to honor the protector is to make your own strength shelter someone weaker.

Story: In saving the elephant king, Vishnu accepts no delay because the cry is sincere; that immediacy illustrates this name.

927 vīrahā वीरहा

Meaning: Destroyer of mighty foes.

Why this name may be here: Names of protection show why devotees trust Vishnu: his greatness is not distant power but power that bends toward preserving dharma.

How we relate: Relate to it by becoming dependable for others; to honor the protector is to make your own strength shelter someone weaker.

Story: The Sudarshana protects Ambarisha from Durvasa's fury, proving this name through preservation of the humble devotee.

928 rakṣaṇaḥ रक्षणः

Meaning: Protector.

Why this name may be here: This name stresses that divine strength is meaningful because it rescues the vulnerable and re-orders what has gone crooked.

How we relate: Relate to it by becoming dependable for others; to honor the protector is to make your own strength shelter someone weaker.

Story: Krishna safeguards Parikshit in the womb from Ashvatthama's weapon; the name fits that unseen guardianship of future dharma.

929 santaḥ सन्तः

Meaning: The peaceful, saintly one.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

930 jīvanaḥ जीवनः

Meaning: Life-giver.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

931 paryavasthitaḥ पर्यवस्थितः

Meaning: Established everywhere around all.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

932 anantarūpaḥ अनन्तरूपः

Meaning: Of endless forms.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

933 anantaśrīḥ अनन्तश्रीः

Meaning: Of infinite splendor.

Why this name may be here: Auspicious names appear to show that Vishnu's majesty is never dry abstraction; it overflows as beauty, grace, and sustaining abundance.

How we relate: Relate to it by caring for beauty, cleanliness, gratitude, and generosity; auspiciousness grows where life is ordered and respectful.

Story: In the story of Andal's longing for Ranganatha, divine beauty becomes an object of total surrender; this name resonates with that magnetic grace.

934 jitamanyuḥ जितमन्युः

Meaning: Conqueror of wrath.

Why this name may be here: Names of protection show why devotees trust Vishnu: his greatness is not distant power but power that bends toward preserving dharma.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: As Mohini, Vishnu protects the devas by recovering the amrita from a chaotic scramble; this name reflects strategic preservation.

935 bhayāpahaḥ भयापहः

Meaning: Remover of fear.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

936 caturaśraḥ चतुरश्रः

Meaning: Perfectly balanced on every side.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

937 gabhīrātmā गभीरात्मा

Meaning: Deep in self.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

938 vidiśaḥ विदिशः

Meaning: Giver of direction.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

939 vyādiśaḥ व्यादिशः

Meaning: Ordainer in all directions.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

940 diśaḥ दिशः

Meaning: The directions themselves.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

941 anādiḥ अनादिः

Meaning: Beginningless.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

942 bhūr bhuvaḥ भूर् भुवः

Meaning: The earth and the mid-region.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: Use it outdoors or in quiet observation; it helps the mind learn contemplation through the senses.

Story: In the forest episodes of the Ramayana, trees, rivers, and mountains become witnesses to Rama's dharma; this name turns the landscape into theology.

943 lakṣmīḥ लक्ष्मीः

Meaning: Auspicious abundance itself.

Why this name may be here: This epithet teaches that prosperity in its pure sense means a life aligned with sacred order, not mere accumulation.

How we relate: Relate to it by caring for beauty, cleanliness, gratitude, and generosity; auspiciousness grows where life is ordered and respectful.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

944 suvīraḥ सुवीरः

Meaning: Of noble heroism.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

945 rucirāṅgadaḥ रुचिराङ्गदः

Meaning: Adorned with shining armlets.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

946 jananaḥ जननः

Meaning: The generator.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

947 janajanmādiḥ जनजन्मादिः

Meaning: Source of the birth of beings.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

948 bhīmaḥ भीमः

Meaning: Terrible to evil.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

949 bhīmaparākramaḥ भीमपराक्रमः

Meaning: Of formidable valor.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Sabari's darshana shows Rama's tenderness toward humble devotion.

950 ādhāranilayaḥ आधारनिलयः

Meaning: Foundation of all support.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

952 puṣpahāsaḥ पुष्पहासः

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

953 prajāgaraḥ प्रजागरः

Meaning: Ever awake.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

954 ūrdhvagaḥ ऊर्ध्वगः

Meaning: Ever-rising, transcendently above.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

955 satpathācāraḥ सत्पथाचारः

Meaning: Walker in the true path.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.
