956 prāṇadaḥ प्राणदः

Meaning: Giver of life.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

957 praṇavaḥ प्रणवः

Meaning: The sacred Om.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

958 paṇaḥ पणः

Meaning: The cosmic exchange or stake.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

959 pramāṇam प्रमाणम्

Meaning: The measure and authority.

Why this name may be here: This epithet reminds the listener that the Lord is not hurried by time because time itself moves within his ordinance.

How we relate: Carry it by living with accountability, because a universe held by order also remembers consequence.

Story: Krishna's promise that dharma is restored age after age frames this name in the long continuity of divine intervention.

960 prāṇanilayaḥ प्राणनिलयः

Meaning: Abode of life-breath.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

961 prāṇabhṛt प्राणभृत्

Meaning: Supporter of life.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

962 prāṇajīvanaḥ प्राणजीवनः

Meaning: Life of life itself.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

963 tattvam तत्त्वम्

Meaning: Reality, truth.

Why this name may be here: Names of insight appear because Vishnu is praised not only as ruler but as the intelligence by which truth becomes knowable.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

964 tattvavit तत्त्ववित्

Meaning: Knower of reality.

Why this name may be here: This epithet signals that wisdom is not separate from God; it is one of the ways his presence becomes inwardly felt.

How we relate: Relate to it by seeking understanding before reaction; devotion matures when insight governs speech and action.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

965 ekātmā एकात्मा

Meaning: The one self in all.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

966 janmamṛtyujarātigaḥ जन्ममृत्युजरातिगः

Meaning: Beyond birth, death, and old age.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

967 bhūrbhuvaḥsvastaruḥ भूर्भुवःस्वस्तरुः

Meaning: The sheltering tree of all the worlds.

Why this name may be here: Names of kindness keep the hymn intimate: the all-pervading Lord is also approachable, patient, and responsive to the heart.

How we relate: It becomes lived when you listen carefully, forgive wisely, and let another person recover dignity in your presence.

Story: Kubja, bent by circumstance, is straightened by Krishna's gracious touch; the name fits a Lord who restores dignity as well as form.

968 tāraḥ तारः

Meaning: The deliverer.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

969 savitā सविता

Meaning: The vivifier.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

970 prapitāmahaḥ प्रपितामहः

Meaning: Ancestor even of the grandsire.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

971 yajñaḥ यज्ञः

Meaning: Sacrifice itself.

Why this name may be here: Such names connect temple, fire, mantra, and surrender into a single vision centered on Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: Bali's gift to Vamana turns a ritual act into total surrender; this name fits the Lord who receives both offering and offerer.

972 yajñapatiḥ यज्ञपतिः

Meaning: Lord of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: The sages at Naimisharanya keep sacrificial time alive through remembrance of Narayana; the name belongs to the divine heart of ritual continuity.

973 yajvā यज्वा

Meaning: Performer of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: In Ambarisha's observance of Ekadashi and Dvadashi, discipline itself becomes worship; this name suits Vishnu as the fulfiller of vowed offering.

974 yajñāṅgaḥ यज्ञाङ्गः

Meaning: Limb of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Such names connect temple, fire, mantra, and surrender into a single vision centered on Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

975 yajñavāhanaḥ यज्ञवाहनः

Meaning: Carrier of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: As Yajna in primordial times, Vishnu upholds cosmic order through sacrificial harmony; the name fits the deity who is both rite and fruit.

976 yajñabhṛt यज्ञभृत्

Meaning: Supporter of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: The Rajasuya of Yudhishthira culminates in Krishna receiving first honors, showing that all offerings find their center in him.

977 yajñakṛt यज्ञकृत्

Meaning: Performer and maker of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Such names connect temple, fire, mantra, and surrender into a single vision centered on Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: Daksha's disrupted sacrifice is restored only when the deeper principle of divine order is honored; this name suits Vishnu as the true sanctifier of ritual.

978 yajñī यज्ञी

Meaning: The one who is worshipped through sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: When Krishna teaches that offerings made with devotion are accepted even if simple, this epithet becomes inward and universal.

979 yajñabhuk यज्ञभुक्

Meaning: Enjoyer of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: Bali's gift to Vamana turns a ritual act into total surrender; this name fits the Lord who receives both offering and offerer.

980 yajñasādhanaḥ यज्ञसाधनः

Meaning: Means by which sacrifice is accomplished.

Why this name may be here: Such names connect temple, fire, mantra, and surrender into a single vision centered on Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: The sages at Naimisharanya keep sacrificial time alive through remembrance of Narayana; the name belongs to the divine heart of ritual continuity.

981 yajñāntakṛt यज्ञान्तकृत्

Meaning: He who brings sacrifice to completion.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes ritual names to say that worship is not a transaction with many powers; its deepest receiver and fulfiller is one Lord.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: In Ambarisha's observance of Ekadashi and Dvadashi, discipline itself becomes worship; this name suits Vishnu as the fulfiller of vowed offering.

982 yajñaguhyam यज्ञगुह्यम्

Meaning: The secret of sacrifice.

Why this name may be here: Sacrificial names remind the listener that every holy offering, outer or inner, finds its completion in Vishnu.

How we relate: Use it to remember that worship is not confined to ritual moments; every sincere act can become an oblation.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

983 annam अन्नम्

Meaning: Food itself.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

984 annādaḥ अन्नादः

Meaning: Eater and giver of food.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

985 ātmayoniḥ आत्मयोनिः

Meaning: Self-born source.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

986 svayaṁjātaḥ स्वयंजातः

Meaning: Self-manifest.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

987 vaikhānaḥ वैखानः

Meaning: One who has dug out or manifested the earth; also linked with Vaikhanasa tradition.

Why this name may be here: This name emphasizes Vishnu's immanence through the textures of lived experience rather than through concept alone.

How we relate: This name is lived when gratitude replaces casual consumption and the natural world is treated as sacred participation, not raw material only.

Story: Varaha lifting the earth from cosmic waters makes elemental imagery literal; this name suits Vishnu as guardian of the world-body.

988 sāmagāyanaḥ सामगायनः

Meaning: Singer of the Sama.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

989 devakīnandanaḥ देवकीनन्दनः

Meaning: Joy of Devaki.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Use this name when the mind feels fragmented; it trains the heart to see unity beneath surface differences.

Story: The Kaliya dance reveals mastery over poison without loss of grace; this story fits many active Krishna epithets.

990 sraṣṭā स्रष्टा

Meaning: Creator.

Why this name may be here: The tradition places such names to remind the listener that the divine is not local or partial but the hidden totality behind all appearance.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

991 kṣitīśaḥ क्षितीशः

Meaning: Lord of the earth.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma includes nature names so the cosmos becomes contemplative material: the world itself can train the eye toward the divine.

How we relate: This name is lived when gratitude replaces casual consumption and the natural world is treated as sacred participation, not raw material only.

Story: Kurma supports Mount Mandara during the churning, turning mountain and ocean alike into instruments of divine purpose.

992 pāpanāśanaḥ पापनाशनः

Meaning: Destroyer of sin.

Why this name may be here: This name stresses that divine strength is meaningful because it rescues the vulnerable and re-orders what has gone crooked.

How we relate: This name becomes practical when you defend truth, keep promises, and stand between harm and the vulnerable.

Story: Narasimha bursts forth to save Prahlada and break tyrannical pride; this name fits the Lord whose protection arrives exactly where hope seems blocked.

993 śaṅkhabhṛt शङ्खभृत्

Meaning: Bearer of the conch.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma closes and intensifies the hymn with martial imagery because Vishnu's compassion sometimes arrives as sharp correction.

How we relate: Carry it by becoming precise in action rather than aggressive in ego.

Story: When Vishnu's many arms bear many implements in iconography, the image teaches that the Lord meets different disorders with fitting remedies.

994 nandakī नन्दकी

Meaning: Bearer of the Nandaka sword.

Why this name may be here: Weapon names are included not to glorify violence but to symbolize precise divine intervention against adharma and inner disorder.

How we relate: Carry it by becoming precise in action rather than aggressive in ego.

Story: Sudarshana pursues Durvasa to defend Ambarisha, showing that divine weaponry protects bhakti rather than egoic ambition.

995 cakrī चक्री

Meaning: Bearer of the discus.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shows that the Lord's instruments are extensions of protection, justice, and awakened order.

How we relate: Carry it by becoming precise in action rather than aggressive in ego.

Story: Krishna's discus severs Shishupala's arrogance at the Rajasuya after long patience, making this name one of measured justice.

996 śārṅgadhanvā शार्ङ्गधन्वा

Meaning: Wielder of the Sharnga bow.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma closes and intensifies the hymn with martial imagery because Vishnu's compassion sometimes arrives as sharp correction.

How we relate: Carry it by becoming precise in action rather than aggressive in ego.

Story: In the war against Narakasura, Vishnu's weapons restore balance by removing predatory power; this name fits that corrective force.

997 gadādharaḥ गदाधरः

Meaning: Bearer of the mace.

Why this name may be here: Weapon names are included not to glorify violence but to symbolize precise divine intervention against adharma and inner disorder.

How we relate: Carry it by becoming precise in action rather than aggressive in ego.

Story: The Sharnga bow in many Purana narratives symbolizes readiness to protect without hesitation; this name extends that image.

998 rathāṅgapāṇiḥ रथाङ्गपाणिः

Meaning: He whose hand bears the wheel.

Why this name may be here: Time-bearing names show that Vishnu is praised as the measure behind change, decay, renewal, and moral consequence.

How we relate: Use this name when restless about results; it teaches that maturation has its season and cannot be bullied into ripeness.

Story: The cyclic creation and dissolution described in the Puranas show kalpas moving within Narayana's will; this name belongs to the Lord behind vast rhythms.

999 akṣobhyaḥ अक्षोभ्यः

Meaning: Unshakable.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

1000 sarvapraharaṇāyudhaḥ सर्वप्रहरणायुधः

Meaning: He whose every implement is a weapon of protection.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma closes and intensifies the hymn with martial imagery because Vishnu's compassion sometimes arrives as sharp correction.

How we relate: Use this name when inner enemies feel stronger than outer ones; it reminds you that divine help can be sharp and cleansing.

Story: Panchajanya, Krishna's conch, announces righteous action and awakens hearts before battle; the name reflects sound as sacred power.

Appendix: Eight concluding namavali names

1001 śaṅkhabhṛte शङ्खभृते

Meaning: To the bearer of the conch.

Why this name may be here: Weapon names are included not to glorify violence but to symbolize precise divine intervention against adharma and inner disorder.

How we relate: Use this name when inner enemies feel stronger than outer ones; it reminds you that divine help can be sharp and cleansing.

Story: Kaumodaki, the mace, stands for solid strength in defense of order; such weapon names remind us that dharma needs force as well as ideals.

1002 nandakine नन्दकिने

Meaning: To the wielder of Nandaka.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shows that the Lord's instruments are extensions of protection, justice, and awakened order.

How we relate: Use this name when inner enemies feel stronger than outer ones; it reminds you that divine help can be sharp and cleansing.

Story: Nandaka, the sword of wisdom, is often read as cutting ignorance; this name suits divine intervention that clarifies as it conquers.

1003 cakriṇe चक्रिणे

Meaning: To the discus-bearing Lord.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma closes and intensifies the hymn with martial imagery because Vishnu's compassion sometimes arrives as sharp correction.

How we relate: Use this name when inner enemies feel stronger than outer ones; it reminds you that divine help can be sharp and cleansing.

Story: When Vishnu's many arms bear many implements in iconography, the image teaches that the Lord meets different disorders with fitting remedies.

1004 śārṅgadhanvane शार्ङ्गधन्वने

Meaning: To the wielder of Sharnga.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma begins and returns often to cosmic names so devotion does not shrink Vishnu to one image; the Lord is the field in which all forms arise.

How we relate: Carry this name by widening your sense of belonging; when the Lord is all-pervading, nothing in life is spiritually irrelevant.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

1005 gadādharāya गदाधराय

Meaning: To the bearer of the mace.

Why this name may be here: Bhishma closes and intensifies the hymn with martial imagery because Vishnu's compassion sometimes arrives as sharp correction.

How we relate: Carry it by becoming precise in action rather than aggressive in ego.

Story: Sudarshana pursues Durvasa to defend Ambarisha, showing that divine weaponry protects bhakti rather than egoic ambition.

1006 rathāṅgapāṇaye रथाङ्गपाणये

Meaning: To the wheel-handed Lord.

Why this name may be here: Time-bearing names show that Vishnu is praised as the measure behind change, decay, renewal, and moral consequence.

How we relate: Carry it by living with accountability, because a universe held by order also remembers consequence.

Story: Krishna delays open confrontation with Kamsa until the right moment, showing time used as instrument rather than obstacle.

1007 akṣobhyāya अक्षोभ्याय

Meaning: To the unshakable Lord.

Why this name may be here: This name widens prayer from a personal request into metaphysical vision: Vishnu is not inside the world only, the world itself rests in him.

How we relate: Relate to it by practicing reverence in ordinary perception: treat work, people, and silence as occurring within the same divine reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

1008 sarvapraharaṇāyudhāya सर्वप्रहरणायुधाय

Meaning: To him whose every instrument is a weapon.

Why this name may be here: This epithet shows that the Lord's instruments are extensions of protection, justice, and awakened order.

How we relate: Use this name when inner enemies feel stronger than outer ones; it reminds you that divine help can be sharp and cleansing.

Story: Krishna's discus severs Shishupala's arrogance at the Rajasuya after long patience, making this name one of measured justice.
